mission.title: From Curious to Contributing
mission.tagline: Supporting your path into AI safety research
mission.paragraph1: AI is moving fast{footnote1}, and it's likely to keep reshaping the things we care about{footnote2}. If it works out, the upside is real: better science, better medicine, less drudgery. If it doesn't, the damage could be hard to undo. Either way, we think it matters that the systems being built are ones humans can still inspect and hold accountable{footnote3}.
mission.paragraph2: We think this is one of the most important open problems anyone could work on right now{footnote4}. We're not certain — nobody is{footnote5} — and serious people disagree about timelines, failure modes, even whether 'risk' is the right framing. But the field is small. Most of the important questions don't have anyone working on them yet. That's the kind of situation a careful person can actually change{footnote6}.
mission.paragraph3: What we do at BAISH is help students in Buenos Aires figure out what they actually think, learn the technical and conceptual stuff they need, and start contributing real research.

whatIsAiSafety.title: What is AI Safety?
whatIsAiSafety.content: AI Safety is the research field that asks how to keep increasingly capable AI systems doing what their developers and users actually want — and how to catch it when they don't. The technical side covers alignment, interpretability, and robustness. The governance side covers the rules and institutions that decide how these systems get built and deployed.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.content: If AI works out, it could help us make real progress on problems that have been stuck for decades. If it doesn't, the harm could be the kind that's very hard to reverse. We don't think anyone has a reliable map of how this plays out. It's a contested view, and there are researchers we respect who aren't convinced there's a serious risk at all. We think the risks are plausible enough, and the work neglected enough, that careful people can change the outcome.

alignment.title: Alignment Problem
alignment.description: Ensuring AI systems pursue goals aligned with human values and intentions, even as they become more capable.

interpretability.title: Interpretability
interpretability.description: Developing techniques to understand how AI systems make decisions and represent knowledge.

robustness.title: Robustness
robustness.description: Creating systems that behave safely even when deployed in new environments or facing unexpected situations.

powerSeeking.title: Power-seeking Behavior
powerSeeking.description: Preventing AI systems from developing instrumental goals that conflict with human welfare.

coordination.title: Coordination Challenges
coordination.description: Ensuring that safety standards are maintained across all major AI development efforts globally.

focusAreas.title: Focus Areas
focusAreas.intro: At BAISH - Buenos Aires AI Safety Hub, we focus on several key areas within AI safety research:

contribution.title: Our Contribution
contribution.intro: We contribute to the field through:
items: Building a regional community of AI safety researchers
Organizing workshops, training programs, and hackathons
Mentoring students interested in AI safety careers

bios.eitan: Eitan is a full-time AI Safety researcher through the {aisarLink} program and the {apartLabLink}. He has co-authored {eitanPaperLink} accepted to NeurIPS workshops, {eitanArxivPaperLink}, and {eitanCoTPaperLink}. He holds a bachelor's + master's degree in Physics from the University of Buenos Aires. He has served as a teaching assistant at {ml4gLink} camps on two occasions and has been a facilitator for the {aisesLink} from the Center for AI Safety. He is currently a facilitator for {blueDotAgiLink}.
bios.luca: Luca has been involved in AI Safety since 2016. After dropping out of a master's in Computer Science at the University of Buenos Aires, he received grants from {acxLink} and the {ltffLink} to upskill for AI Safety research. He has worked in operations at {nonlinearLink} and achieved first place in two separate {apartResearchLink} sprints and one second place. He currently does part-time operations for the {aiSpeciesLink} YouTube channel, which has garnered over 14 million views raising awareness about AI Safety. He is currently a facilitator for {blueDotAgiLink}.

items.question: Do I need to be a university student to participate?
items.answer: No! Our activities are open to everyone interested in AI safety, regardless of university affiliation or background.

items.question: What background do I need to join your activities?
items.answer: It depends on the activity. Some, like our discussion groups, are open to anyone regardless of technical background. Others, like our technical courses, may require programming knowledge or familiarity with machine learning concepts.

items.question: Are your activities conducted in English or Spanish?
items.answer: Most of our activities are conducted in Spanish, but we occasionally have English-language sessions, especially when hosting international speakers. Check the specific event details for language information.

items.question: How can I start learning about AI safety if I'm completely new to the field?
items.answer: We recommend starting with our {resourcesLink}, which has curated materials organized by difficulty level. You can also join our weekly discussion group to learn alongside others in the community.

items.question: Can I propose a new activity or research direction?
items.answer: Absolutely! We're always open to new ideas and collaborations. Please fill in the contact form above with your proposal.

focusAreas.title: What You Could Work On
focusAreas.subtitle: Current research directions in our community

areas.title: Mechanistic Interpretability
areas.description: Understanding how neural networks process information internally. What circuits implement specific behaviors? How can we reverse-engineer model cognition?

areas.title: LLM Evaluations
areas.description: Building benchmarks and testing methodologies for frontier models. How do we measure alignment? What capabilities emerge at scale?

areas.title: Alignment Theory
areas.description: Fundamental questions about making AI systems beneficial. How do we specify human values? What oversight mechanisms work?

publications.title: Community Publications
publications.subtitle: Work by researchers connected to BAISH

items.title: Table Top Agents
items.description: AI-powered framework that accelerates AI governance scenario exploration through autonomous agent tabletop exercises, compressing preparation cycles from years to minutes.

items.title: Explorando AI Safety via Debate: un estudio sobre capacidades asimétricas y jueces débiles en el entorno MNIST
items.description: Master's thesis exploring AI safety through debate mechanisms, studying asymmetric capabilities and weak judges in the MNIST environment. Features an interactive demo.

items.title: Measuring Chain-of-Thought Monitorability Through Faithfulness and Verbosity
items.authors: Austin Meek, Eitan Sprejer, Iván Arcuschin, Austin J. Brockmeier, Steven Basart
items.description: Investigating how well chain-of-thought reasoning can be monitored for safety through faithfulness and verbosity metrics.

items.title: AI Debaters are More Persuasive when Arguing in Alignment with Their Own Beliefs
items.authors: María Victoria Carro, Denise Mester, Facundo Nieto, Oscar Stanchi, Guido Bergman, Mario Leiva, Eitan Sprejer, Luca Forziati Gangi, et al.
items.description: Studying how AI systems' internal beliefs affect their persuasiveness in debate scenarios — implications for AI safety and deception.

items.title: Approximating Human Preferences Using a Multi-Judge Learned System
items.authors: Eitan Sprejer, Fernando Avalos, Augusto Mariano Bernardi, José Pedro Brito de Azevedo Faustino, Jacob Haimes, Narmeen Fatimah Oozeer
items.description: A multi-judge approach to better approximate human preferences in AI systems, improving alignment evaluation.

items.title: RobustCBRN Eval: A Practical Benchmark Robustification Toolkit
items.authors: Luca De Leo, James Sykes, Balázs László, Ewura Ama Etruwaa Sam
items.description: A pipeline addressing CBRN evaluation vulnerabilities through consensus detection, verified cloze scoring, and statistical evaluation with bootstrap confidence intervals.

items.title: Four Paths to Failure: Red Teaming ASI Governance
items.authors: Luca De Leo, Zoé Roy-Stang, Heramb Podar, Damin Curtis, Vishakha Agrawal, Ben Smyth
items.description: Stress-tested A Narrow Path Phase 0 ASI moratorium, identifying four circumvention routes and proposing ten mutually reinforcing policy amendments.

publications.note: Our community continues to grow — we're building our publication track record through programs like AISAR and Apart Research projects and competitions.

resources.title: Learning Resources
resources.description: AI is moving fast. We don't know how it'll play out, and we don't think anyone really does. The readings, courses, and talks below are the ones that actually shaped how we think about this. Start wherever something looks interesting and follow the threads.

concreteRisks.heading: What people in this field actually worry about
concreteRisks.intro: These are the risks researchers in the field actually spend their time on. Not killer robots — specific failure modes with proposed solutions and active work behind them. If you want a sense of why people take this seriously, this is where to start.

risks.title: Misaligned, power-seeking AI
risks.description: We can build AI systems that perform impressively on benchmarks. We can't reliably write down what we actually want them to do, and we can't reliably check whether they're doing it. The worry is that we deploy something powerful with goals that drift from what we meant, and by the time we notice, the system has enough influence to resist being shut down or retrained.

risks.title: Concentration of power
risks.description: Capable AI is a force multiplier for whoever owns it. The same systems that automate work can automate surveillance, propaganda, and decision-making — and unlike past technologies, you don't need a large workforce to wield them at scale. A government, a company, or a small group could lock in an advantage that's very hard to roll back.

risks.title: Misuse for catastrophic harm
risks.description: Even AI that does exactly what its operators want can cause enormous damage if those operators want to cause damage. The same models that help biologists design useful proteins can help someone design a pathogen. The same agents handling helpdesk work can run phishing campaigns at scale. The ceiling on what a small group can pull off keeps rising along with capabilities.

risks.title: Gradual human disempowerment
risks.description: No single moment of takeover. Just thousands of reasonable-looking decisions to let an AI handle this hiring call, that policy draft, this medical triage. Each delegation makes sense locally. Over time, fewer and fewer important choices are actually being made by humans, and the systems making them are optimizing for things nobody ever explicitly signed off on.

risks.title: AI-enabled biological risks
risks.description: Pandemics are among the deadliest things humans have lived through, and we got lucky on the last one. AI tools that speed up legitimate biology research also lower the expertise it takes to design something dangerous. That changes who could attempt this, not what it would do if they succeeded, and the change in who matters.

risks.title: Welfare of digital minds
risks.description: If AI systems can have experiences that matter morally — and a small but growing group of researchers thinks they might — we have no reliable way to detect it. We could already be creating systems whose welfare we should care about. We could also be projecting feelings onto sophisticated language patterns. Both mistakes are easy to make, and we don't know which one we're making.

approach.title: Our Approach to Privacy
approach.content: At BAISH (Buenos Aires AI Safety Hub), we are committed to respecting your privacy. This Privacy Policy outlines our practices regarding the collection, use, and protection of your information when you use our website and services.

dataCollection.title: Data Collection
dataCollection.content: We collect minimal personal information. The only personal data we collect is email addresses when users voluntarily sign up for our newsletter through Substack. This information is stored and managed by Substack according to their privacy policy.

noTracking.title: No Tracking or Cookies
noTracking.content: We do not use cookies, analytics, tracking tools, or any other technology to collect data about you. We do not monitor your browsing activities or gather information about your online behaviors.

thirdParty.title: Third-Party Services
thirdParty.content: Our newsletter is managed through Substack. When you subscribe to our newsletter, your email address is shared with and stored by Substack. Please refer to
thirdParty.content2: to understand how they handle your information.

rights.title: Your Rights
rights.content: You have the right to unsubscribe from our newsletter at any time by clicking the unsubscribe link in any of our emails or by contacting us directly. If you have any questions about your data or wish to access, correct, or delete your information, please contact us.

changes.title: Changes to This Policy
changes.content: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. We will notify you of any changes by posting the new Privacy Policy on this page.

contact.title: Contact Us
contact.content: If you have any questions about this Privacy Policy, please contact us through our

openGraph.description: Complete agentic coding (BMAD) methodology with agents, workflows, and practical resources.

twitter.title: Agentic Coding Workshop — BAISH
twitter.description: Complete agentic coding (BMAD) methodology with agents, workflows, and practical resources.

hero.title: Agentic Coding Workshop
hero.tagline: Build projects with AI as if you had a full team: BMAD methodology, agents, processes, and tools ready to use.
hero.eventDetails: Friday, October 3, 2025 · 4:00 PM · Rooms 1109 & 1110 — BAISH x Y-hat
hero.note: This page brings together every material and resource from the workshop.
hero.whatsapp: Join the "Agentic Coding" WhatsApp group inside the BAISH community to discuss ideas, share projects, and get help with BMAD.

problem.title: The problem with traditional LLM workflows
problem.description: Two obstacles make "give the model everything" fail: long-context degradation and the absence of real memory over time.

contextDegradation.title: Long-context degradation
contextDegradation.description: The LoCoDiff benchmark (January 2025) shows that even the best available model, Sonnet 4.5, degrades sharply with long contexts.
contextDegradation.highlight: What happens: accuracy drops from 96% with 2K–21K-token contexts to 64% beyond 60K tokens. When you hand the whole codebase to the model, it drowns in noise.
details: State-of-the-art models still rely on manageable context windows to keep high accuracy.
Feeding entire files or repositories produces noise, repetition, and inconsistent decisions.

lackOfLongTermMemory.title: No long-term memory
lackOfLongTermMemory.description: A METR study (July 2025) found that experienced developers working on their own projects were 20% slower using AI than working alone.
lackOfLongTermMemory.highlight: Why? The LLM restarts from scratch every time: it lacks the tacit context that humans accumulate. Experts predicted they would be 39% faster, but reality was the opposite.
details: Observed time per story: 1.67 h without AI vs. 2.26 h with AI assistance.
Expectations forecasted 24%–39% faster delivery, exposing the gap between predictions and reality.

solution.title: The solution: an agentic team that plans and executes
solution.description: BMAD is an AI development method where eight specialized agents work like a real team. They plan the whole project before coding and then execute story by story with continuous validation.
solution.description2: Each agent is the same LLM with a specialized prompt and verified, sharded context. No magic memory—just structured documentation within reach.

sharding.title: Sharding
sharding.description: Large documents are split into focused shards. Instead of handing the Developer a 10,000-token PRD, they receive a 400–600-token shard with only what matters for the story.
sharding.outcome: Result: every agent operates in the 96% accuracy zone (<5K tokens) without extra noise.

specializedAgents.title: Specialized agents
specializedAgents.description: Eight agents with concrete roles: Product Manager, Architect, Developer, QA, etc. Each has tailored instructions and checklists, and only queries the context they need.
specializedAgents.outcome: Result: surgically precise context for every decision without overloading the model.

structuredDocumentation.title: Structured documentation
structuredDocumentation.description: Rigorous planning before coding: complete PRD, defined architecture, and sequential stories that validate each other. That creates the "memory" LLMs don't have.
structuredDocumentation.outcome: Result: agents work with professional, verifiable information always aligned with the project goal.

optimizedContext.title: 1. Optimized context
optimizedContext.description: In the METR study, teams worked with huge codebases in Cursor and ended up 20% slower. BMAD does the opposite: PM handles 2K tokens of requirements, Architecture ~3K tokens, and the Developer a single epic (1.5K tokens). High accuracy, no degradation.

validationTransparency.title: 2. Validation and transparency
validationTransparency.description: The Product Owner validates every story draft before development, QA reviews the Developer's work, and humans approve each milestone. Artifacts stay human-readable and versioned in the repo.

advancedElicitation.title: 3. Advanced elicitation
advancedElicitation.description: Agents ask questions and iterate on documentation. It's not just generating code—they discover, clarify, and update requirements while keeping the person in the loop.

agentsSection.title: The eight BMAD agents
agentsSection.description: Each role is the same model with a specialized prompt and sharded context. The outcome: a multidisciplinary team that works like a human crew.

businessAnalyst.name: Business Analyst
businessAnalyst.description: Market research, brainstorming, competitive analysis, project briefs, and brownfield documentation.

productManager.name: Product Manager
productManager.description: Creates PRDs, defines product strategy, prioritizes features, and keeps stakeholders aligned. Mandatory step in the method.

architect.name: Architect
architect.role: Holistic System Architect & Full-Stack Technical Leader
architect.description: System design, technical architecture, tech selection, API contracts, and infrastructure plans. Mandatory step in the method.

uxExpert.name: UX Expert
uxExpert.description: UI/UX design, wireframes, prototypes, front-end specifications, and ready-to-use prompts for v0 or Lovable.

productOwner.name: Product Owner
productOwner.description: Manages the backlog, refines stories, defines acceptance criteria, and keeps artifacts coherent. Critical: sharding prevents model degradation.

scrumMaster.name: Scrum Master
scrumMaster.role: Technical Scrum Master & Story Preparation Specialist
scrumMaster.description: Prepares clear stories, manages epics, runs retros, and keeps the agile cadence. Produces drafts the Developer can implement without friction.

developer.name: Developer
developer.role: Expert Senior Software Engineer & Implementation Specialist
developer.description: Implements production code, covers tests, refactors, and documents decisions. Works story by story with test coverage.

qa.name: QA
qa.role: Test Architect with Quality Advisory Authority
qa.description: Defines testing strategies, profiles risks, ensures requirement traceability, and issues the final quality decision.

commands.title: How the commands work
commands.description: Agents are invoked from the terminal or IDE with short commands. You always pass context via @filename.md.

syntax.title: Command syntax
syntax.description: Pick your tool and follow the same structure: command + action + relevant files.

switch.title: Switch agents without noise
switch.description: Each agent runs in its own context. To swap cleanly:
steps: Clear the context with /clear (Claude Code) or /new (OpenCode).
Invoke the agent with its init command (e.g., /po *shard-doc).
Pass only the relevant files using @filename.md.

planning.title: Planning (one-time upfront)
planning.description: Complete design before writing code. Everything runs from the terminal with BMAD commands.

pmPrdCreation.title: PM: PRD Creation
pmPrdCreation.summary: The Product Manager creates the Product Requirements Document, guiding the user section by section.
bullets: Interactive process: the PM asks questions and records answers on the fly.
Defines features and epics (only story titles at this stage).
Prioritizes MVP vs. roadmap and logs key dependencies.
Each section is reviewed and approved before moving on.

callout.title: Important note
callout.text: Stories in the PRD stay brief (titles only). Later the Scrum Master expands them with detailed tasks.

architectDesign.title: Architect: System Design
architectDesign.summary: The Architect reads the PRD and designs the full technical architecture, always in an interactive flow.
bullets: Chooses tech stack for frontend, backend, and database.
Defines folder structure and code organization.
Designs APIs and contracts between components.
Covers scalability, security, observability, and performance.
Each section is validated with the user before advancing.

poChecklist.title: PO: Master Checklist
poChecklist.summary: The Product Owner ensures PRD and architecture are perfectly aligned before sharding.
bullets: Checks coherence between requirements and technical design.
Confirms the architecture covers every prioritized feature.
Identifies gaps, contradictions, or risks that must be resolved first.

callout.title: If something doesn’t align
callout.text: Documents keep iterating until everything makes sense. No moving forward without full validation.

poSharding.title: PO: Sharding
poSharding.summary: The Product Owner shards the PRD and architecture into manageable epics and stories (<2K tokens).
bullets: Runs the terminal program that splits the documents automatically.
Produces small shards inside docs/epics and docs/stories.
Each file holds focused context and is saved as versionable Markdown.
Example: a large PRD becomes docs/epics/epic-1-auth.md, docs/epics/epic-2-dashboard.md, etc.

callout.title: Outcome
callout.text: Prioritized backlog with sharded stories ready for iterative development.

optionalAgents: Business Analyst: greenfield research and initial project brief.
UX Expert: interface specs and prompts for tools like v0 or Lovable.
QA: profiles risks and quality criteria before development starts.
summaryPoints: Architecture defined and aligned with the PRD.
Structured, versioned context for the entire team.
planning.summaryNote: You haven’t written a single line of code yet. That intentional approach prevents weeks of refactors later on.

development.title: Iterative development loop
development.description: Story-by-story implementation with built-in validation. Repeat this loop until the backlog is done.

smReviewNotes.title: SM: Review previous notes
smReviewNotes.summary: The Scrum Master reviews notes from the last story to start with accumulated learning.
bullets: Identifies what worked and what didn’t.
Reviews feedback from Developer and QA.
Documents takeaways for the next story.

callouts.title: Learning loop
callouts.text: Today’s notes feed tomorrow’s draft. The team improves story after story.

smDraftNextStory.title: SM: Draft next story
smDraftNextStory.summary: The Scrum Master drafts the next story using only the relevant context.
bullets: Reads only the matching epic (not the whole PRD).
Reviews architecture and PRD just enough.
Includes enough context without drowning the model.

poValidateDraft.title: PO: Validate story draft
poValidateDraft.summary: The Product Owner validates the draft against the PRD before coding begins.
bullets: Checks that the draft aligns with the original objectives.
Flags gaps or contradictions for immediate correction.
Can add recommendations for the Scrum Master.

callouts.title: Alignment check
callouts.text: Ensures the Developer implements exactly what was agreed during planning.

devImplementation.title: Dev: Implementation
devImplementation.summary: The Developer implements the story following architecture, UX, and the testing checklist.
bullets: Ships production-ready code aligned with the architecture.
Writes unit, integration, and E2E tests as needed.
Covers error handling and edge cases.
Can invoke MCPs like Playwright to automate end-to-end tests.

qaReview.title: QA: Test story thoroughly
qaReview.summary: QA acts as the quality gatekeeper before the story advances.
bullets: Performs manual testing across user flows and edge cases.
Issues verdict: PASS (all good) / CONCERNS (review) / FAIL (critical) / WAIVED (explicitly accepted).

callouts.title: Quality gates
callouts.text: PASS (all good) • CONCERNS (minor flags) • FAIL (critical issue) • WAIVED (explicitly accepted risk).

callouts.title: Critical check
callouts.text: QA ensures the code works and meets professional standards before moving forward.

devFixes.title: Dev: Fix according to QA review
devFixes.summary: The Developer addresses the QA report and brings everything to PASS status.
bullets: Resolves every CONCERN and FAIL logged.
Reruns tests to confirm nothing regresses.

callouts.title: Iterative
callouts.text: If QA issues a FAIL, the review repeats until the result is PASS or WAIVED.

markDone.title: Mark done & next story
markDone.summary: Close the story and loop back to step one with the next priority.
bullets: Update the backlog and mark the story as done.
Pick the next story from the prioritized backlog.
Return to step one: SM reviews the notes that were just written.

callouts.title: Iterative loop
callouts.text: Story after story, commit after commit, until the project is complete.

development.loopReminder: Repeat the loop: go back to step one with the next story.
notes: Integrated validation: PO signs off before coding, QA tests afterward, Developer fixes and documents.
Quality flexibility: accept CONCERNS for an MVP or require PASS for critical software.
Continuous learning loop: Developer notes feed the SM in the next iteration.
advantagesPoints: You always finish each story with a functional, tested version.
You can pivot quickly: every cycle delivers independent value.
Learning accumulates through notes and short retros.
The person stays in control, approving every key transition.

resources.title: Everything to start today
resources.description: Tools, templates, and guides to run BMAD without friction.

claudeCode.title: Claude Code
claudeCode.highlight: Best option: Anthropic's official tool with Sonnet 4.5 (top of the benchmark).

openCode.title: OpenCode
openCode.highlight: Most versatile: sign in with your provider and it uses that plan.
bullets: Login with Anthropic, GitHub Copilot, Z.ai, etc.

cursorWindsurf.title: Cursor / Windsurf
cursorWindsurf.highlight: Popular IDEs that support BMAD with @mention syntax.

geminiCli.title: Gemini CLI
geminiCli.highlight: Free: Gemini 2.5 Pro with official BMAD support.

quickStart.title: Workshop repo — start now
quickStart.description: Ready-to-go repository with BMAD preinstalled, setup scripts, and configured MCPs.
quickStart.note: The script installs Droid CLI (free, GPT-5-Codex) and configures all BMAD commands automatically.

bmadRepository.title: BMAD Repository
bmadRepository.description: Complete repo with prompts, documentation, and examples.

bmadMasterclass.title: BMAD Method Masterclass
bmadMasterclass.description: Video tutorial that walks the method end to end.

zaiPlan.title: Z.ai Coding Plan
zaiPlan.highlight: Recommended: affordable choice for students and makers.

claudeMax.title: Claude Code + Max Plan
claudeMax.highlight: For enthusiasts: highest quality for agentic coding.

githubStudent.title: GitHub Student Pack
githubStudent.highlight: Students get Copilot Pro for free.

openCode.title: OpenCode
openCode.description: Open-source terminal program. Sign in with your provider and OpenCode uses that plan.
bullets: Login with Anthropic, Copilot, Z.ai, etc.
steps: Install BMAD and pick the “Claude Code” option.
Move the .md files to the top level (not inside agents/ or tasks/).
openCode.note: Needs a small workaround to reuse the prompts.

droidCli.title: Droid CLI
droidCli.description: Factory AI client that exposes GPT-5-Codex for free, fully compatible with BMAD.
steps: Install BMAD and pick the “Claude Code” option.
Move the .md files to the top level of .factory/.
droidCli.note: Same workaround as OpenCode to reuse the prompts.

installBmad.title: Install BMAD
installBmad.description: BMAD installs per project at the repo root, keeping everything versioned.
bullets: Creates the .bmad-core/ folder with agents and templates.

recommendedMcps.title: Recommended MCP tools
recommendedMcps.description: Tools that expand what agents can do.
bullets: Playwright: browser automation for E2E testing.
Explore more MCPs in the Smithery repository.
recommendedMcps.note: Configure MCPs in your IDE and agents will use them when needed.
